title: - - POWERS OF LAYERS FOR IMAGE TO IMAGE TRANSLATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - - POWERS OF LAYERS FOR IMAGE TO IMAGE TRANSLATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We propose a simple architecture to address unpaired image-to-image translation tasks: style or class transfer, denoising, deblurring, deblocking, etc. We start from an image autoencoder architecture with fixed weights. For each task we learn a residual block operating in the latent space, which is applied iteratively until the target domain is reached. A specific training schedule is required to alleviate the exponentiation effect of the iterations. At test time, it offers several advantages: the number of weight parameters is limited and the strength of the transformation can be modulated simply with the number of iterations. This is useful, for instance, when the type or amount of noise to suppress is not known in advance. Experimentally, we show that the performance of our model is comparable or better than CycleGAN and Nice-GAN with fewer parameters.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

stacking layers, as supported by the universal approximation theorem (Hornik et al., 1989; Montúfar, 2014). More precisely, the theorem states that stacking a number of basic blocks can approximate any function with arbitrary precision, provided it has enough hidden units, with mild conditions on the non-linear basic blocks. Studies on non-linear complex holomorphic functions involved in escape-time fractals showed that iterating simple non-linear functions can also construct arbitrarily complex landscapes (Barnsley et al., 1988). These functions are complex in the sense that their iso-surfaces are made arbitrarily large by increasing the number of iterations. Yet there is no control on the actual shape of the resulting function. This is why generative fractals remain mathematical curiosities or at best tools to construct intriguing landscapes.

In this paper, our objective is to combine the expressive power of both constructions, and we study the optimization of a function that iterates a single building block in the latent space of an auto-encoder. We focus on image translation tasks, that can be trained from either _paired_ or _unpaired_ data. In the paired case, pairs of corresponding input and output images are provided during training. It offers a direct supervision, so the best results are usually obtained with these methods (Chen et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019). We focus on **unpaired translation** : only two corpora of images are provided, one for the input domain _A_ and the other for the output domain _B_ . Therefore we do not have access to any parallel data (Conneau et al., 2017), which is a realistic scenario in many applications, e.g., image restoration. We train a function _fAB_ : _A ! B_ , such that the output _b[⇤]_ = _F_ ( _a_ ) for _a 2 A_ is indiscernible from images of _B_ .

Our transformation is performed by a single residual block that is composed a variable number of times. We obtain this compositional property thanks to a progressive learning scheme that ensures that the output is valid for a large range of iterations. As a result, we can modulate the strength of the transformation by varying the number of times the transformation is composed. This is of particular interest in image translation tasks such as denoising, where the noise level is unknown at training time, and style transfer, where the user may want to select the best rendering. This “Powers of layers” (PoL) mechanism is illustrated in Figure 1 in the category transfer context (horse to zebra).

Our architecture is very simple and only the weights of the residual block differ depending on the task, which makes it suitable to address a large number of tasks with a limited number of parameters. This proposal is in sharp contrast with the trend of current state-of-the-art works to specialize the architecture and to increase its complexity and number of parameters (Fu et al., 2019; Viazovetskyi et al., 2020; Choi et al., 2020). Despite its simplicity, our proof of concept exhibits similar or better performance than a vanilla CycleGAN architecture, all things being equal otherwise, for the

Figure 1: Illustration of Powers of layers for a category transfer task. The encoder and decoder are directly borrowed from a vanilla auto-encoder and are not learnable. At inference time, we apply a variable number of compositions, producing different images depending on how many times we compose the residual block in the embedding space. Depending on the task, we either modulate the transformation and choose the result, or let a discriminator determine when to stop iterating.

original set of image-to-image translation tasks proposed in their papers, as well as for denoising, deblurring and deblocking. With significantly fewer parameters and a versatile architecture, we report competitive results confirmed by objective and psycho-visual metrics, illustrated by visualizations. We will provide the implementation for the sake of reproducibility.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Generative adversarial networks (GANs)** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) is a framework where two networks, a generator and a discriminator, are learned together in a zero-sum game fashion. The generator learns to produce more and more realistic images w.r.t. the training dataset. The discriminator learns to separate between real data and increasingly realistic generated images. GANs are used in many tasks such as domain adaptation (Bousmalis et al., 2016), style transfer (Karras et al., 2019b), inpainting (Pathak et al., 2016) and talking head generation (Zakharov et al., 2019).

**Unpaired image-to-image translation** considers the tasks of transforming an image from a domain _A_ into an image in a domain _B_ . The training set comprises a sample of images from domains _A_ and _B_ , but no pairs of corresponding images. A classical approach is to train two generators ( _A ! B_ and _B ! A_ ) and two discriminators, one for each domain. When there is a shared latent space between the domains, a possible choice is to use a variational auto encoder like in CoGAN Liu & Tuzel (2016). CycleGAN Zhu et al. (2017), DualGAN Yi et al. (2017) and subsequent works (Liu et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020) augment the adversarial loss induced by the discriminators with a cycle consistency constraint to preserve semantic information throughout the domain changes. All these variants have architectures roughly similar to CycleGAN: an encoder, a decoder and residual blocks operating on the latent space. They also incorporate elements of other networks such as StyleGAN Karras et al. (2019a). In our work, we build upon a simplified form of the CycleGAN architecture that generalizes over tasks easily. We applied also our method to NICE-GAN Chen et al. (2020). A concurrent work, GANHopper by Lira et al. (2020) proposes to iterate CycleGAN generators in order to perform the transformation. However, their method has many difference with ours: They iterate full generators and not a single residual block, their encoder and decoder are not fixed, their number of iteration is fixed and they have to use additional discriminators to act on intermediate transformation states. Other works (Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020) use recurrent networks to perform transformations but this is done in a paired context, which results in very different methods from ours and GANHopper.

**Transformation modulation** is an interpolation between two image domains. It is a byproduct of some approaches:For instance, a linear interpolation in latent space (Brock et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2015) morphs between two images. Nevertheless, one important limitation is that the starting and ending points must both be known, which is not the case in unpaired learning. Other approaches such as the Fader networks (Lample et al., 2017) or StyleGan2 (Viazovetskyi et al., 2020) act on scalar or boolean attributes that are disentangled in the latent space ( _eg.,_ age for face images, wear glasses or not, etc). Nevertheless, this results in complex models, for which dataset size and the variability of

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

images strongly impacts the performance: they fail to modulate the transform with small datasets or with large variabilities. A comparison of PoL with the Fader network is provided in Appendix C and shows that our approach is more effective.

**Progressive learning and inference time modulation** are performed in multi-scale methods such as SinGAN (Rott Shaham et al., 2019) and ProgressiveGAN (Karras et al., 2017). Progressive learning obtains excellent results for high resolution images where it is more difficult to use classical approaches. The training is performed in several steps during which the size of both the images and the network are increased. The inference time of some architectures can be modulated by stopping the forward pass at some layer (Huang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017; Veit & Belongie, 2017).This differs from our approach, where the number of residual block compositions (“powers”) can be chosen, to shorten the inference. A by-product is a reduction of the number of network parameters.

**Weight sharing** is a way of looking at our method, because the same layer is applied several times within the same network. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) are the most classical example weight sharing in a recursive architecture. Besides RNNs, weight sharing is mainly used for model compression, sequential data and ordinary differential equations (ODE) (Gao et al., 2019; Han et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2018).A few works (Jastrzebski et al., 2017; Zhang & Jung, 2018) apply weight sharing to unfold a ResNet and evaluate its performance in classification tasks. The optimization is inherently difficult, so they use independent batch normalization for each shared layer. With PoL we observe the same optimization issues, that we solve by a progressive training strategy, see Section 3.3. Recent work (Jeon et al., 2020) are interested in the composition of the same block by considering the parallel with the fixed point theorem, nevertheless their application remains to rather simple problems compared to our unpaired image-to-image translation tasks.

## 3 POWER OF LAYERS

We adopt the same context as CycleGANand focus on unpaired image-to-image translation: the objective is to transform an image from domain _A_ into an image from domain _B_ . In our case the domains can be noise levels, painting styles, blur, JPEG artifacts, or simply object classes that appear in the image. The training is unpaired: we do not have pairs of corresponding images at training time. CycleGAN is simple, adaptable to different tasks and allows a direct comparison in Sections 4 and 5.

We learn two generators and two discriminators. The generator _GAB_ : _I ! I_ transforms an element of _A_ into an element of _B_ , and _GBA_ goes the other way round, _I_ being the fixed-resolution image space. The discriminators _DA_ : _I !_ [0 _,_ 1] (resp. _DB_ ) predicts whether an element belongs to domain _A_ (resp _B_ ). We use the same losses as commonly used in unpaired image-to-image translation:

_L_ Cyc( _GAB, GBA_ ) = E _b⇠B_ [ _kGAB_ ( _GBA_ ( _b_ )) _− bk_ 1] + E _a⇠A_ [ _kGBA_ ( _GAB_ ( _a_ )) _− ak_ 1] _, L_ Id( _GAB, GBA_ ) = E _b⇠B_ [ _kGAB_ ( _b_ ) _− bk_ 2] + E _a⇠A_ [ _kGBA_ ( _a_ ) _− ak_ 2] _._

The Adversarial loss _L_ Adv( _GAB, DB_ ) verifies that the generated images are in the correct domain. The Cycle Consistency loss _L_ Cyc( _GAB, GBA_ ) ensures a round-trip through the two generators reconstructs the initial image, and the identity loss _L_ Id( _GAB, GBA_ ) penalizes the generators transforming images that are already in their target domain. We keep the same linear combination coefficients as in CycleGAN: _λ_ Adv = 1, _λ_ Cyc = 10, _λ_ Id = 5.

## 3.1 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

We start from the CycleGAN architecture.The encoder and decoder consist of 2 layers and a residual block. The embedding space _E_ of our model is 256 _⇥_ 64 _⇥_ 64: its spatial resolution is 1/4 the input image resolution of 256 _⇥_ 256 and it has 256 channels. All translation operations take place in the fixed embedding space _E_ . The encoder Enc : _I ! E_ produces the embedding and consists of two convolutions. The decoder Dec : _E ! I_ turns the embedding back to image space and consists of two transposed convolutions.
**Pre-training of the auto-encoder.** We train the encoder and decoder of our model on a reconstruction task using an _`_ 2 reconstruction loss in pixel space. We use the Adam optimizer. Our

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

data-augmentation consists of an image resizing, a random crop and a random horizontal flip. Both the encoder and decoder weights are fixed for all the other tasks, only the residual block is adapted (and the discriminator in case we use it for the stopping criterion).

There are two choices to train the auto-encoder: (1) train with 6M unlabeled images randomly drawn from the YFCC100M dataset (Thomee et al., 2016) during one epoch, which makes it independent of the translation training dataset; (2) train on the dataset of our unpaired image-to-image translation task. For simplicity, we choose the first option and use a single encoder and decoder for all the tasks presented in this article. For the NiceGAN experiments, the encoder and decoder are trained jointly with the discriminator, so the auxiliary dataset YFCC100M is not used.

**The embedding transformer – single block.** The transformation between domains is based on a residual block _fAB_ , similar to the feed-forward network used in transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017). It writes:

There is a dimensionality expansion factor _K_ between the two convolutions in the residual block (see Figure 1). Adjusting _K_ changes the model’s capacity. We adopt the now standard choice of the original transformer paper ( _K_ = 4). The full generator writes

The other direction, with _fBA_ and res _BA_

**Powers of layers.** We start from the architecture above and augment its representation capacity. There are two standard ways of doing this: (1) augmenting the capacity of the _fAB_ block by increasing _K_ ; (2) increasing the depth of the network by chaining several instances of _fAB_ , since the intermediate representations are compatible. In contrast to these fixed architectures, PoL _iterates_ the _fAB_ block _n ≥_ 1 times, which amounts to sharing the weights of a deeper network:

## 3.2 OPTIMIZATION IN A RESIDUALS BLOCKS WEIGHT SHARING CONTEXT

In the following, we drop the _AB_ suffix from _fAB_ , since powers of layers operates in the same way on _fAB_ and _fBA_ . Thus, _f_ : _E ! E_ is _f_ ( _x_ ) = _x_ + res( _x_ ). The parameters of _f_ are collected in a vector _w_ . The embedding _x 2 E_ is a 3D activation map, but for the sake of the mathematical derivation we linearize it to a vector. The partial derivatives of _f_ are _[@] @x[f]_[=] _[@] @x_[res][+ Id][ and] _@w[@][f]_[=] _[@] @w_[res][.] We compose the _f_ function _n_ times as

The stability of the SGD optimization depends on the magnitude and conditioning of the matrix:

Indeed, the length of the SGD steps on _w_ depends on the eigenvalues of _Mn_ . When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation _L 2_ R _[d][⇥][d]_ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have _Mn_ = ( _L_ + Id) _[n][−]_[1] . The eigenvalues of _Mn_ are ( _λi_ + 1) _[n][−]_[1] , where _λ_ 1 _, ..., λd_ are the eigenvalues of _L_ . At initialization, the components of _L_ are sampled from a random uniform distribution. To reduce the magnitude of _λi_ , one option is to make the entries of _L_ small. However, to decrease ( _λi_ + 1) _[n][−]_[1] sufficiently, _λi_ must be so small that it introduces floating-point cancellations when the residual block is added back to the shortcut connection. This is why we prefer to adjust _n_ , as detailed next.

## 3.3 PROGRESSIVE TRAINING

We adopt a progressive learning schedule in a “warm up” phase: we start the optimization with a single block and add one iteration at every epoch until we reach the required _n_ blocks. This is

Table 1: Denoising: PSNR on Urban-100 Huang et al. (2015). Comparison between Power of layers (POL) and independent (ind) blocks for different maximum number of composition / residual blocks. The best value for each column is in **Bold** . We could not fit more than 16 independent blocks in memory in our experiments. Appendix A reports standard deviations and more results.

possible because the blocks operate in the same embedding space _E_ and because their weights are shared, so all blocks are still in the same learning schedule. This approach avoids the numerical exponentiation effects at initialization mentioned in the previous paragraph. After the warm-up phase, the gradient descent starts to converge, so these numerical effects become unlikely. In addition, this approach allows the discriminator to improve progressively during the training. For example, in the case of the transformation horse _!_ zebra, a slightly whitened horse fools the discriminator at the beginning of the training, but a texture with stronger stripes is required later on.

**Training for modulation.** If the network is trained with a fixed number of compositions, the intermediate states do not correspond to modulations of the transformation that “look right” (see Appendix A). Therefore, in addition to this scheduled number of iterations during the first _n_ epochs of warm-up, we also randomize the number of iterations in subsequent epochs. This forces the generator to also produce acceptable intermediate states, and enables modulating the transform.

**Stopping criterion at inference time.** Each image of domain _A_ is more or less close to domain _B_ . For example, when denoising, the noise level can vary so the denoising strength should adapt to the input. Similarly for horse _!_ zebra: a white horse is closer to a zebra than a brown horse. Therefore, at inference time, we can adjust the number of compositions as well. In particular, for each test image, we select _n_ that best deceives the discriminator, thus effectively adapting the processing to the relative distance to the target domain.

## 4 ANALYSIS

In this section we study the impact of the main training and design choices and on the performance of powers of layers. Appendices A and B provide complementary analysis for training- and inferencetime choices, respectively. For this preliminary analysis, we focus on denoising tasks for which the performance is easily measurable. We add three types of noise to images: Gaussian noise, Gaussian blur and JPEG artifacts. The noise intensity is quantified by the noise standard deviation, the blur radius and the JPEG quality factor, respectively. We generate transformed images and measure how well our method recovers the initial image. This provides a more reliable accuracy measure than a purely unpaired evaluation, that will be carried out in the experimental section.

Note that, in the literature, these tasks are best addressed by providing (original, noisy) pairs if images. Our objective is to remain in a completely unpaired setting during the training phase. It corresponds to the case where parallel data is not available (like for the restoration of ancient movies), and also better reflects the situation where the noise strength is not known in advance. Therefore, the original image is solely employed to measure the performance.

**Experimental protocol.** To train, we sample 800 domain _A_ images from the high-resolution Div2K dataset (Agustsson & Timofte, 2017). In the baseline training procedure, the warm-up phase starts from a single block and increases the number of compositions at every epoch, until we reach epoch _n_ tr. Then we keep the number of compositions fixed.

We test on the Urban-100 (Huang et al., 2015) dataset. Unless specified otherwise, we set the number of compositions to _n_ te = _n_ tr and measure the Peak Signal to Noise Ratio (PSNR) of our model on the dataset images, degraded with the same intensity as at training time. For the JPEG case we use the Naturalness Image Quality Evaluator metric (NIQE, lower=better) instead, because it is more sensitive to JPEG artifacts (Mittal et al., 2013). NIQE is a perceptual metric that does not take the original image into account (i.e. for JPEG we emphdo use an unpaired metric).
**Block composition or independent successive blocks?** Table 1 compares PoL’s composition of the same block versus using independent blocks with distinct weights. In spite of the much larger

Figure 2: Response of the discriminators as a function of the number of compositions for the transformation Gaussian noise _!_ natural image. We plot the average response and the standard deviation over examples (gray). Higher=the discriminator classifies the image into its target domain. _Top:_ training with a fixed number of compositions, _n_ tr=30. _Bottom:_ training with randomised _n_ tr _2_ [[20 _,_ 30]].
capacity offered by independent blocks, the noise reduction operated by Power of layers is stronger. Our interpretation is that the model is easier to train.

**Analysis of the progressive training strategy.** Table 1 also evaluates the impact of the maximum number of compositions _n_ tr. Having several compositions clearly helps. Since we choose the number of compositions _n_ te at inference time (see next paragraph), it may be relevant to vary _n_ tr at training time to minimize the discrepancy between the train- and test-time settings. For this, we tried different intervals to randomly draw the maximum number of compositions for each epoch, after the warm-up phase. If _n_ te is fixed, the optimal choice is _n_ tr = _n_ te. However, if we use an adaptive _n_ te, the best range is _n_ tr _2_ [[20 _,_ 30]], and the adaptive case with randomised training gives the best performance for denoising and debluring. Appendix A reports results obtained with different _n_ tr ranges.

**Stopping criterion.** We consider two cases: either we use a fixed _n_ te, or we use the discriminator to evaluate the transformation quality: it selects the value _n_ te maximizing the target discriminator error for a given image. Figure 2 shows that setting a fixed _n_ tr causes the discriminator to select _n_ te = _n_ tr as the best iteration at inference time. By selecting the best _n_ te for each image we obtain on average a PSNR improvement of +1.36dB for a Gaussian noise of standard deviation 30, compared to fixing _n_ te. In Appendix B, we compare it with the best possible stopping criterion: an oracle that selects _n_ te directly on the PSNR. Our adaptive strategy significantly tightens the gap to this upper bound.

**Comparison with CycleGAN.** We use CycleGAN as a baseline. The differences between CycleGAN and powers of layers are (1) we use a single encoder and decoder trained in advance and common to all tasks; (2) CycleGAN has 9 residual blocks, PoL iterates a single residual block an arbitrary number of times. The inference time of PoL depends on the number of compositions _n_ te but the number of parameters does not:

Figure 3 compares the performance obtained by the two methods on denoising tasks with varying noise intensities. PoL gives better results than CycleGAN in terms of objective metrics, and overall the images produced by our method look as realistic and/or accurate.

**Training with few images.** Figure 3 also compares our method with CycleGAN when training in a data-starving scenario. Whatever the number of training images, PoL outperforms CycleGAN, but the gap is particularly important with very few images. This is expected for two reasons. Firstly, our approach has fewer parameters to learn than CycleGAN. Secondly, it only requires to learn the transformation because the encoder and decoder are pre-learned as a vanilla auto-encoder, while CycleGAN needs to learn how to encode and decode images.

**Parametrization: remarks.** Beyond the settings inherited from CycleGAN, the main training parameters of Powers of layers are the maximum number of compositions _n_ tr and the range from which they are randomly sampled. The number of compositions at inference time _n_ te is also important but the discriminator criterion can be used to set it automatically.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We now run experiments on two image generation applications. We refer to Section 3.1 for the architecture and training protocol. In Appendix C we also give a comparison with the Fader network for the capacity to modulate a transformation, and more visual examples in Appendix D.

**Unpaired image-to-image translation.** We report results for 6 of the 8 unpaired image-to-image translation tasks introduced in the CycleGAN (Zhu et al., 2017) paper (the two remaining ones lead to the same conclusions) and we used the datasets from their website. We compare the Frechet Inception Distance (FID) of CycleGAN, NiceGAN and our approach applied to this method in Figure 4. The FID measures the similarity between two datasets of images, we use it to compare the target dataset with the transformed dataset. It is a noisy measure for which only large deviations are significant. Yet the results and visualization show that our method has results comparable to those of CycleGAN and NiceGAN, achieved with much fewer parameters.

**High resolution experiments.** PoL is fully convolutional architecture, therefore it is technically possible to apply models trained in a low resolution to high resolution images. However, the results

7

Figure 5: _Left:_ Different visual results with high resolution images. See the original images in Appendix D. _Right:_ Comparisons of the generations obtained by models trained either with high-resolution or low-resolution images, applied to a high-resolution test image.
Figure 6: Evolution of the optimal number of composition according to the noise. The maximum number of compositions used for training is 30 and the Gaussian Noise standard deviation is 30.

are not always convincing, as shown in Figure 5 (right) where the model trained on low resolution images does not create stripes at the “right” scale on zebras. To circumvent this problem, CycleGAN trains on patches taken from high resolution images. This works for transformations affecting the whole image (painting _$_ photo), but this is not applicable in the case where only a part of the image is affected (horse _!_ zebra). In contrast, our proposal can adapt the memory used by changing its number of compositions, so we can apply it to very large images without running out of memory. Figure 5 (left) depicts results obtained with our method trained on high resolution images.

**Adjusting** _n_ te **at inference time.** Each image is more or less distant from the target domain, so we explore adapting the transformation to each image rather than applying a fixed transformation. For example, depending on the amount of noise, we may want to adjust the strength of the denoising. By modulating _n_ te we can adapt the transformation to each image. Figure 6 shows that the more noisy the input image is, the more we should compose to best denoise with Powers-of-Layers.

In the same way, Figure 7 shows the progressive transformations obtained on different unpaired image-to-image translation tasks. It shows that progressive transformations are realistic for most tasks.

Figure 7: Illustration of the different results obtained along the iterations of the recurrent block, during the transformation of a horse into a zebra. The first image is the original image, then each image corresponds to 5 additional compositions of our method. Since our method was learned with a maximum of 30 compositions the last two images are extrapolations.

**Combining transformation.** The different blocks associated with different transformations operate in the same embedding space for different tasks. Hence we can compose transformations, each being realized by one residual block. We train Transform #1 in the usual way, then freeze its residual block. Transform #2 is trained on the output of #1. Visual results are in Figure 8. The composition in the embedding space gives better results than decoding/encoding to image space mid-way.

## 6 CONCLUSION

Powers of layers iterates a residual block to learns a complex transformation with no direct supervision. On various tasks, It gives similar performance to CycleGAN and NiceGAN with fewer parameters. The flexibility offered by the common embedding space allows the modulation of the transformation strength, or to compose several transformations. While in most examples the discriminator is only used for training, Powers of layers can also exploit it to adjust the transformation to the input image at inference time.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

Composition in embedding space Composition in image space
Original +denoising +to zebra +denoising +to zebra
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 8: Composition of transformations in the embedding/image space.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to unpaired image-to-image translation by iterating a single shared residual block in a fixed latent space. While the conceptual idea is clear and the empirical results (FID, PSNR) are competitive with baselines like CycleGAN, there is a significant mathematical discrepancy regarding the derivation of the gradient/stability matrix in Section 3.2. Furthermore, certain architectural details—specifically regarding Batch Normalization and the adaptive stopping criterion—are underspecified, which risks reproducibility issues given that weight-sharing in deep ResNets is known to be numerically unstable without specific normalization strategies.

comments.title: Incorrect derivation of the Jacobian/Stability Matrix
comments.quote: When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation $L \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$... we have $M_n = (L + Id)^{n-1}$. The eigenvalues of $M_n$ are $(\lambda_i + 1)^{n-1}$
comments.explanation: The authors define $M_n$ as the matrix whose eigenvalues determine SGD stability for the weight vector $w$. However, if we assume a linear residual block $f(x) = x + Lx = (I+L)x$, the $n$-th composition is $f^{(n)}(x) = (I+L)^n x$. When calculating the gradient with respect to the parameters within $L$, the result should involve a geometric sum of the transformation matrices, not just the $(n-1)$-th power of the operator. Specifically, for a linear layer where $L$ is effectively the parameter, the derivative of $(I+L)^n x$ with respect to $L$ follows the rule for powers of matrices, leading to $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (I+L)^k x (I+L)^{n-1-k}$. The simplified expression $(L+Id)^{n-1}$ only appears in the chain rule for the input $x$ (the Jacobian), not necessarily the parameter gradient which governs 'the length of SGD steps on $w$'. This is a technical error in the derivation of the optimization stability analysis.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding Normalization Layers in shared blocks
comments.quote: f(x) = x + res(x) ... The optimization is inherently difficult, so they use independent batch normalization for each shared layer. With PoL we observe the same optimization issues, that we solve by a progressive training strategy
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors acknowledge that previous works (Jastrzebski et al., 2017) required independent Batch Normalization (BN) parameters for each step of an unfolded ResNet to succeed. In Section 3.1 and 3.3, the authors imply they solve these issues via progressive training but do not explicitly state whether the BN layers in their residual block share parameters across iterations or use iteration-specific statistics/parameters. If parameters are strictly shared (as the 'single block' claim suggests), BN is notoriously difficult to converge because the feature distribution shifts with each iteration. If they use iteration-specific BN, the 'fixed number of parameters' claim is slightly undermined. The current description is too vague for exact reproduction of the weight-sharing mechanism.

comments.title: Possible OCR error or notation inconsistency in discriminator loss
comments.quote: the output $b[*] = F(a)$ for $a \in A$ is indiscernible from images of $B$.
comments.explanation: The symbol $b[*]$ appears only once and seems to be a notation for the generated image, but elsewhere the paper uses $G_{AB}(a)$. Given the context of OCR mentioned in the prompt, this is likely an OCR misread of $b^*$. However, if intended, it is undefined relative to the standard $G(a)$ notation used in the rest of Section 3.

comments.title: Unspecified 'Stopping Criterion' during training
comments.quote: Depending on the task, we either modulate the transformation and choose the result, or let a discriminator determine when to stop iterating.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest using a discriminator to decide $n_{te}$ at inference. It is unclear if this discriminator is the same one trained during the GAN process (which typically sees only the final output or a specific range of iterations) or a specialized one. More importantly, Figure 2 shows the discriminator response for $n_{tr}$ up to 30, but if the discriminator was only trained on 'real' vs 'generated at $n=n_{tr}$', its behavior for $n < n_{tr}$ is not guaranteed to be monotonic or meaningful for a stopping criterion without specific 'label smoothing' or multi-step training. The paper does not specify if the discriminator is trained on all intermediate steps $1 \dots n$ as 'fakes' or only the final step.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Parameter Count Table
comments.quote: encoder + decoder 1x1.7M | residual block 2xKx1.1M | total 15.9M
comments.explanation: The table in Section 4 lists 'residual block' as $2 \times K \times 1.1M$. With $K=4$ (as stated in Section 3.1), this would be $8.8M$. Adding the encoder/decoder ($1.7M$) and two discriminators ($2 \times 2.7M = 5.4M$) yields $8.8 + 1.7 + 5.4 = 15.9M$. However, in the 'Total' column for CycleGAN, it lists $28.2M$. It is unclear if the $15.9M$ total for PoL includes both directions ($A \to B$ and $B \to A$). If PoL shares the same residual block for both directions, it should be stated; if not, the count for 'residual block' should likely be doubled. The comparison is slightly obscured by how $K$ is factored into the final sum.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and conceptually elegant framework—Powers of Layers (PoL)—that leverages iterative application of a shared residual block in latent space for unpaired image-to-image translation. The method is well-motivated, with strong connections to weight sharing, residual networks, and progressive training strategies. The experimental evaluation is thorough, covering denoising, deblurring, deblocking, and style transfer, and the results support the claim of competitive performance with fewer parameters than CycleGAN and NiceGAN. The idea of modulation via iteration count is particularly valuable for real-world applications where noise level or style intensity is unknown. However, there are a few technical and logical concerns, primarily around the derivation of gradient dynamics and a subtle inconsistency in training protocol description, which need clarification.

comments.title: Incorrect eigenvalue expression for Jacobian matrix in gradient analysis
comments.quote: The eigenvalues of _Mn_ are ( _λi_ + 1) _[n][−]_[1] , where _λ_ 1 _, ..., λd_ are the eigenvalues of _L_ .
comments.explanation: The paper analyzes gradient stability in the weight-sharing setting by approximating the residual block as a linear map _L_, leading to _f(x) = x + Lx = (I + L)x_. The composition of _n_ such blocks gives a transformation whose Jacobian with respect to the input is (I + L)^n. The gradient with respect to weights, however, involves a sum over compositions: the correct expression for the gradient matrix _Mn_ should be _Mn = Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} (I + L)^k_, as each residual connection contributes independently during backpropagation through time. The paper instead claims the eigenvalues are (λ_i + 1)^{n−1}, which would be relevant for the Jacobian of the full transformation, not the accumulated gradient. This mischaracterizes the gradient explosion/vanishing dynamics. While the qualitative point—that eigenvalues grow exponentially and cause optimization difficulty—is valid, the quantitative derivation is flawed. This affects the justification for progressive training as a stabilization mechanism.

comments.title: Inconsistency between training protocol and Table 1 caption
comments.quote: Table 1: Denoising: PSNR on Urban-100 Huang et al. (2015). Comparison between Power of layers (POL) and independent (ind) blocks for different maximum number of composition / residual blocks.
comments.explanation: The caption suggests that the POL (Power of Layers) and independent (ind) blocks are compared across different maximum numbers of compositions/residuals. However, the text in Section 4 states that, due to memory constraints, no more than 16 independent blocks could be fit. The table includes entries for _n_tr_ = 30 for both POL and ind, which contradicts this. It is possible that the 'ind' results at n=30 are mislabeled or extrapolated, but given the explicit memory limitation, including such a row is misleading. The discrepancy suggests either the table includes infeasible experiments or the caption/text is imprecise. Given that reproducibility is key, this numerical inconsistency undermines confidence in the fairness of the comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the full generator composition
comments.quote:
The full generator writes

There is a dimensionality expansion factor _K_ between the two convolutions in the residual block
comments.explanation: The sentence "The full generator writes" is followed by a paragraph break and no equation, which is clearly a formatting or OCR error. While the intended meaning—defining the full generator as iterative application of the residual block in latent space—is clear from context (e.g., Figure 1 and surrounding text), the absence of a formal expression for _GAB_ = Dec ◦ fAB^n ◦ Enc is a missed opportunity. In a technical paper, especially one centered on iterative composition, a precise formula here would prevent ambiguity. However, since the concept is well-explained elsewhere and the omission does not lead to misinterpretation, this is a minor issue. It may also stem from PDF extraction problems rather than author error.

comments.title: Misleading claim about pre-training dataset independence in NiceGAN experiments
comments.quote: For the NiceGAN experiments, the encoder and decoder are trained jointly with the discriminator, so the auxiliary dataset YFCC100M is not used.
comments.explanation: The sentence suggests that in NiceGAN experiments, the encoder/decoder are trained from scratch on the task data, which would undermine the claim of parameter efficiency and shared architecture. However, this contradicts the paper’s core premise of a fixed, pre-trained encoder-decoder. The more plausible interpretation is that ‘NiceGAN experiments’ refer to replacing CycleGAN with NiceGAN as the baseline, while PoL still uses the pre-trained autoencoder. The phrasing is ambiguous and could mislead readers into thinking the architectural advantage (shared encoder-decoder) is abandoned in those experiments. With clarification, the issue resolves, but as written, it risks misrepresenting experimental consistency.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to image-to-image translation using iterated residual blocks in latent space. The core idea of modulating transformation strength through iteration count is novel and potentially useful. However, there are several technical issues that undermine confidence in the method's theoretical foundation and some aspects of the experimental evaluation. The mathematical derivation in Section 3.2 contains a significant error, and some key claims lack proper justification. While the empirical results are promising, the technical issues need to be addressed.

comments.title: Incorrect Jacobian derivation for linear residual block
comments.quote: When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation $L \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have $M_n = (L + \text{Id})^{n-1}$. The eigenvalues of $M_n$ are $(\lambda_i + 1)^{n-1}$, where $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d$ are the eigenvalues of $L$.
comments.explanation: This derivation is mathematically incorrect. For a linear residual block f(x) = x + L(x), we have f^n(x) = x + nL(x) (since higher-order terms vanish for linear operations). Therefore, the Jacobian ∂(f^n)/∂x = I + nL, not (I + L)^{n-1} as claimed. The eigenvalues would be 1 + nλ_i, not (1 + λ_i)^{n-1}. This error undermines the theoretical justification for the optimization difficulties discussed in this section. The authors may have confused the composition of linear maps with the binomial expansion that would apply for nonlinear residual connections.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about numerical effects after convergence
comments.quote: After the warm-up phase, the gradient descent starts to converge, so these numerical effects become unlikely.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that numerical instabilities related to exponentiation effects disappear after the network converges, but provide no justification for this assertion. The numerical issues arise from the eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix at initialization, and convergence does not inherently change these spectral properties. Without a formal analysis or empirical evidence showing how convergence mitigates these numerical effects, this claim remains unsubstantiated. The authors should either remove this claim or provide a proper mathematical or empirical justification.

comments.title: Ambiguous justification for discriminator-based stopping criterion
comments.quote: Each image of domain A is more or less close to domain B. For example, when denoising, the noise level can vary so the denoising strength should adapt to the input. Similarly for horse → zebra: a white horse is closer to a zebra than a brown horse. Therefore, at inference time, we can adjust the number of compositions as well. In particular, for each test image, we select n that best deceives the discriminator, thus effectively adapting the processing to the relative distance to the target domain.
comments.explanation: The connection between 'deceiving the discriminator' and optimal transformation strength is not adequately justified. The discriminator is trained to distinguish between real and generated samples, but maximizing its error doesn't necessarily correspond to optimal reconstruction quality. For denoising, for instance, the discriminator might prefer over-smoothed images that lack realistic texture but have high discriminator confidence. The authors should provide evidence (e.g., correlation analysis) that maximizing discriminator error indeed corresponds to better reconstruction or translation quality.

comments.title: Insufficient pre-training details
comments.quote: We train the encoder and decoder of our model on a reconstruction task using an $\ell_2$ reconstruction loss in pixel space. We use the Adam optimizer. Our data-augmentation consists of an image resizing, a random crop and a random horizontal flip.
comments.explanation: The pre-training of the autoencoder is critical to the method's success, but key details are missing. What learning rate was used? How many epochs? What was the final reconstruction loss? The authors mention training on 6M images from YFCC100M for 'one epoch' - this seems insufficient for such a large dataset and could affect the quality of the learned latent space. Without these details, reproducibility is compromised and it's difficult to assess whether the encoder/decoder quality is a limiting factor.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for residual functions
comments.quote: The transformation between domains is based on a residual block $f_{AB}$, similar to the feed-forward network used in transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017). It writes: $f_{AB}(x) = x + \text{res}(x)$ ... In the following, we drop the AB suffix from fAB, since powers of layers operates in the same way on fAB and fBA. Thus, f : E → E is f(x) = x + res(x).
comments.explanation: The paper uses both $f_{AB}$ (with subscript) and $f$ (without subscript) to refer to the residual block function. While the authors explain they drop the suffix for simplicity, this creates potential confusion. In some places, $f_{AB}$ appears to refer specifically to the A→B transformation, while $f$ refers to the general concept. This inconsistency could mislead readers about whether the same function is being discussed throughout the paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous parameter counting in comparison
comments.quote: |encoder + decoder|residual block|discriminators|total\n|1×1.7M|2×K×1.1M|2×2.7M|15.9M|\n|2×11.4M|2×2.7M|28.2M||
comments.explanation: The parameter count table is confusing. For PoL, it shows '2×K×1.1M' for the residual block, but K=4 (from Section 3.1), so this would be 8.8M. The total shown is 15.9M, but 1.7M + 8.8M + 5.4M = 15.9M only if K=3.5. Similarly for CycleGAN, 2×11.4M + 5.4M = 28.2M, but the table header suggests this should include 9 residual blocks. The math doesn't add up and the table layout is unclear about what each row represents.

comments.title: Unclear composition of transformations
comments.quote: The different blocks associated with different transformations operate in the same embedding space for different tasks. Hence we can compose transformations, each being realized by one residual block. We train Transform #1 in the usual way, then freeze its residual block. Transform #2 is trained on the output of #1.
comments.explanation: The composition of transformations is not clearly explained. If Transform #1 maps domain A to B, and Transform #2 is trained on the output of #1, does Transform #2 map B to C? Or does it map A to C directly? The description suggests sequential training but doesn't clarify the domain relationships. Also, since each block is trained for specific domain pairs, composing them requires careful consideration of whether the intermediate domain matches the training distribution.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting and computationally efficient approach to unpaired image-to-image translation by iterating a single residual block in a fixed latent space. The methodology is well-motivated and the empirical results demonstrate competitive performance with a significantly smaller parameter footprint. The primary issues identified relate to the mathematical presentation of the weight-sharing gradient stability and some minor ambiguity in the definition of the residual block components relative to the literature.

comments.title: Mathematical Derivation of Gradient Stability
comments.quote: The partial derivatives of f are [@] @x[f]_[=] _[@] @x_[res][+ Id][ and] _@w[@][f]_[=] _[@] @w_[res][.] We compose the f function n times as f_n(x) = f(f(...f(x)...))
comments.explanation: The notation describing the derivatives is mangled by OCR artifacts (e.g., [@] @x[f]_[=] _[@] @x_[res][+ Id]). More importantly, when expressing the partial derivative of the n-fold composition with respect to the weights 'w', the chain rule implies a summation of terms involving all intermediate states f_k(x). The text treats 'Mn' as a matrix of eigenvalues, but it doesn't clearly define 'Mn' in terms of the Jacobian product chain, which is critical for understanding the stability of the optimization. While the qualitative intuition regarding the spectral radius of the derivative is standard, the formal grounding for the claim that 'the length of the SGD steps... depends on the eigenvalues of Mn' is obscured by the formulaic notation breakdown.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Residual Block Definition
comments.quote: The transformation between domains is based on a residual block fAB, similar to the feed-forward network used in transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017). It writes: res AB Encoder Decoder Stopping criterion Discriminator CONV RELU NORM CONV
comments.explanation: The text references a residual block structure, but the defining equation for 'res' is missing. The figure text lists components (CONV RELU NORM CONV), but the sequence/order (e.g., Pre-Norm vs. Post-Norm) and the placement of the skip connection are critical to the 'Powers of Layers' mechanism and for reproducing the results. Given the emphasis on the simplicity of the transformation, the lack of an explicit formal definition for the block 'res' or the total function 'f' makes the specific architecture slightly ambiguous.

comments.title: Notation drift in function definitions
comments.quote: The full generator writes f_n(x) = f(f(...f(x)...))
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the authors define f as a building block and then introduce n-fold composition. They then state 'The full generator writes' but leave the equation unfinished before transitioning to the discussion of f_BA and res_BA. While the intent—that the generator is the n-fold composition of the residual block within the encoder-decoder pipeline—is clear from the context, the mathematical definition of the full generator as a composition of operators (Enc, Dec, and the iterated res) is never formally closed.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a simple and compelling idea for unpaired image-to-image translation: iterating a single residual block in a frozen autoencoder's latent space. The core concepts (weight sharing, progressive training, and inference-time modulation) are clearly motivated. The main strengths are the method's parameter efficiency and its ability to modulate transformation strength at test time. However, the paper has several significant issues. The mathematical analysis of gradient stability in Section 3.2 contains a potential notational error in the indexing of the Jacobian product (Mn), which could mislead readers about the exact form of the gradient. More critically, the analysis of the stopping criterion using the discriminator is underdeveloped; while Figure 2 shows the discriminator's response, the paper does not provide a clear, reproducible algorithm for how this criterion is applied at test time, which is a key claimed advantage. The claims about performance being 'comparable or better' than CycleGAN and NiceGAN are generally supported by the FID scores in Figure 4, but the advantage is marginal and varies significantly across tasks, which the text does not fully address. The comparison in Figure 3 (top) uses 'CLG' as shorthand for CycleGAN, and its relationship to the CycleGAN baseline discussed in the text is not explicitly clarified. Overall, the work is a solid proof of concept with clear novelty, but its quantitative evaluation could be more rigorous and some methodological steps require better specification.

comments.title: Potential index error in gradient stability analysis (Jacobian product M_n)
comments.quote: Indeed, the length of the SGD steps on $w$ depends on the eigenvalues of $M_n$ . When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation $L \in \mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have $M_n = (L + Id)^{n-1}$ .
comments.explanation: The text defines the iterative application as $G^n(x) = f^n(x)$ and then discusses the Jacobian $\frac{\partial G^n}{\partial w} = M_n \frac{\partial f}{\partial w}$, where $M_n = \prod_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}[f^{k-1}(x)]$. For the linear case, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = L + Id$. The product is then $(L+Id)^{n-1}$. However, the text writes $M_n = (L+Id)^{n-1}$, which is consistent with the derivation if the product starts at $k=1$. The potential issue is whether the exponent $n-1$ is correct or if it should be $n$. Since $G^n$ is defined as $n$ compositions, the chain rule product has $(n-1)$ factors of the Jacobian of $f$ (evaluated at the intermediate states). For the linear case, this is indeed $(L+Id)^{n-1}$. So the formula is technically correct. However, the text in the paragraph preceding the quote states 'The partial derivatives of $f$ are $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}[f] = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}[res] + Id$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial w}[f] = \frac{\partial}{\partial w}[res]$.' This uses a non-standard partial derivative notation which is confusing. The subsequent explanation that 'The length of the SGD steps on $w$ depends on the eigenvalues of $M_n$' is also an oversimplification; it depends on the full Jacobian, not just $M_n$, which multiplies $\frac{\partial f}{\partial w}$. The text's conclusion that the analysis leads to the progressive training strategy is reasonable, but the derivation is presented in a sloppy manner.

comments.title: Ambiguous stopping criterion at inference time
comments.quote: Therefore, at inference time, we can adjust the number of compositions as well. In particular, for each test image, we select $n$ that best deceives the discriminator, thus effectively adapting the processing to the relative distance to the target domain.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the discriminator can be used at test time to select the optimal number of iterations, which is a key selling point. However, the description is insufficient. It is not clear how 'best deceives the discriminator' is operationalized. Does it mean selecting the $n$ that maximizes $D_B(G_{AB}^n(a))$ (i.e., the discriminator's belief that the output is from domain B)? Or does it mean minimizing it? For a 'deceive' objective, one would expect the former. Figure 2 supports this by showing the discriminator's response as a function of iterations. However, the paper does not specify if the discriminator is fine-tuned or used as trained, what the search range for $n$ is, or if this selection is done per image or per batch. This lack of detail makes the method difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: Unclear baseline abbreviation in quantitative comparison
comments.quote:
Impact of the noise intensity applied to Urban-100 images
Noise    Noisy    Denoising    Sigma    Blur    Debluring    JPEG    JPEG    Deblocking
(std)    images    CLG    PoL    Blur    images    CLG    PoL    quality    images    CLG    PoL
comments.explanation: In Figure 3 (top), the abbreviation 'CLG' is introduced without definition in the caption or the surrounding text. Given the context of the paper, this almost certainly refers to CycleGAN. The authors later refer to 'CycleGAN' explicitly in the text and in other figures. While an experienced reader can infer this, the inconsistency is a minor oversight. The table header also shows a column for 'Denoising' which then lists 'CLG' and 'PoL' under 'Noise (std)', and a column for 'Debluring' (misspelled). This makes the table less readable than it should be. The abbreviation should be defined, and the spelling of 'Deblurring' should be corrected.

comments.title: Oversimplified claim about gradient stability based solely on linear analysis
comments.quote: This approach avoids the numerical exponentiation effects at initialization mentioned in the previous paragraph. After the warm-up phase, the gradient descent starts to converge, so these numerical effects become unlikely.
comments.explanation: The paper's analysis of gradient stability in Section 3.2 is entirely linear (ignoring ReLU and normalization). While the progressive training strategy is a sensible practical heuristic, the text claims that this resolves the issue derived from the linearized analysis. The paper does not discuss how non-linearities (e.g., ReLU clipping gradients, batch norm changing the effective Jacobian) might affect this analysis. The claim that 'these numerical effects become unlikely' after convergence is vague and not supported by empirical evidence beyond the reported results. The reasoning is insufficient to fully justify the effectiveness of the warm-up phase.

comments.title: Unclear relationship between 'ind' and 'POL' in Table 1
comments.quote: Table 1: Denoising: PSNR on Urban-100 Huang et al. (2015). Comparison between Power of layers (POL) and independent (ind) blocks for different maximum number of composition / residual blocks.
comments.explanation: The table effectively demonstrates that PoL (weight sharing) outperforms using independent blocks ('ind') of equivalent total depth. However, the caption states 'independent (ind) blocks' but does not specify the training procedure for the independent case. Are they trained jointly or sequentially? Are they trained with the same progressive schedule? The text in Section 4 only says 'versus using independent blocks with distinct weights.' It is ambiguous whether the 'ind' baseline uses a single encoder/decoder or is a separate architecture. For a fair comparison, the only difference should be weight sharing vs. no weight sharing, but this is not explicitly confirmed. This makes the primary conclusion of the table somewhat less rigorous.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting and conceptually simple method for unpaired image-to-image translation by iteratively applying a single residual block in a latent space (Powers of Layers). The core idea of modulating the transformation strength by varying the number of iterations is strong, and the experimental results against CycleGAN are compelling, particularly regarding parameter efficiency. However, the paper contains a mathematical error in the justification for the training difficulty (an off-by-one error in the exponent of a matrix power derivation) and provides parameter counts in a table whose raw text is so garbled by OCR that it prevents verification of the architecture's complexity. These issues prevent full confidence in the technical derivations presented.

comments.title: Mathematical error in Jacobian derivation for iterative residual blocks
comments.quote: When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation $L \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have $M_n = (L + Id)^{n-1}$.
comments.explanation: The authors define $f(x) = x + res(x)$ and, under linearization, $f(x) = (I + L)x$. Composing this function $n$ times results in $f^n(x) = (I + L)^n x$. Consequently, the Jacobian or sensitivity matrix governing the gradient flow should be $(I+L)^n$. The text claims the matrix is $(I+L)^{n-1}$. While the qualitative conclusion—that deeper networks (higher $n$) are more sensitive to optimization—remains true regardless of the off-by-one error, the specific derivation of the eigenvalues and the mathematical justification for the need for progressive training (to alleviate the exponentiation effect) is technically incorrect.

comments.title: Garbled parameter breakdown in Figure 3 table
comments.quote: Table caption and text within Figure 3 OCR output: `|encoder + decoder 1_⇥_1.7M |2_⇥K ⇥_1_._1M |2_⇥_11.4M |discriminators | 2_⇥_2.7M | 2_⇥_2.7M | |total |15.9M | 28.2M | |`
comments.explanation: The parameter counts presented in Figure 3 are critical for supporting the paper's claim of superior parameter efficiency. However, the OCR text for the table rows (`1_⇥_1.7M`, `2_⇥K ⇥_1_._1M`) is incoherent and impossible to reconstruct into standard parameter counting (e.g., millions of parameters). While the total values (15.9M for PoL and 28.2M for CycleGAN) are internally consistent with the text's claim that PoL has fewer parameters, the failure to provide a readable breakdown of the components (encoder, decoder, residual block, discriminators) makes this data unverifiable for the reader.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a parameter-efficient image-to-image translation method (Powers of Layers) that iterates a single residual block in a fixed latent space. The core idea is conceptually sound and builds naturally on weight-sharing and residual architectures. However, the manuscript suffers from several reproducibility gaps (missing training schedule details, garbled/missing equations due to OCR), a shaky mathematical justification for the proposed progressive training strategy, and an overstatement of performance relative to NiceGAN in the abstract and experiments. With clarified derivations, corrected performance claims, and explicit hyperparameter settings, the work would be stronger.

comments.title: Underspecified randomization of iteration counts during training
comments.quote: Therefore, in addition to this scheduled number of iterations during the first n epochs of warm-up, we also randomize the number of iterations in subsequent epochs.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the number of iterations is randomized to enable inference-time modulation, but they do not specify the range or probability distribution from which these iterations are sampled. A careful reader cannot reproduce the training schedule without knowing whether iterations are drawn uniformly from [1, n_tr], weighted, or sampled from a custom distribution. Specifying this sampling procedure is essential for reproducibility and for understanding how it affects convergence and the discriminator's role.

comments.title: Overclaim regarding NiceGAN performance comparison
comments.quote: Experimentally, we show that the performance of our model is comparable or better than CycleGAN and Nice-GAN with fewer parameters.
comments.explanation: The abstract and Section 5 claim that PoL is 'comparable or better than CycleGAN and Nice-GAN'. However, Figure 4 clearly shows that PoL achieves higher (worse) FID scores than NiceGAN on 5 out of 6 translation tasks (e.g., Summer→Winter: 46.1 vs 40.5; Horse→Zebra: 53.0 vs 44.8; Van-Gogh→Picture: 134.4 vs 101.4). The only task where PoL is slightly better is Apple→Orange. The claim is therefore misleading and overstates the empirical results. The abstract should be tempered to reflect that PoL is competitive with CycleGAN and more parameter-efficient, while NiceGAN still leads on standard FID metrics.

comments.title: Hand-wavy derivation of gradient scaling and SGD stability
comments.quote: When simplifying the basic residual block to a linear transformation L 2_ R _[d][⇥][d]_ (i.e., ignoring the normalization and the ReLU non-linearity), we have _Mn_ = ( _L_ + Id) _[n][−]_[1] . The eigenvalues of _Mn_ are ( _λi_ + 1) _[n][−]_[1] , where _λ_ 1 _, ..., λd_ are the eigenvalues of _L_ . At initialization, the components of _L_ are sampled from a random uniform distribution. To reduce the magnitude of _λi_ , one option is to make the entries of _L_ small. However, to decrease ( _λi_ + 1) _[n][−]_[1] sufficiently, _λi_ must be so small that it introduces floating-point cancellations when the residual block is added back to the shortcut connection.
comments.explanation: The paper skips the chain-rule derivation linking composition depth n to the gradient scaling matrix Mn. While the form (I+L)^(n-1) is standard for the Jacobian of a repeated residual block w.r.t. its weights, the justification for why this causes optimization instability is unclear. Specifically, the claim that reducing λi to control (1+λi)^(n-1) causes 'floating-point cancellations' is numerically imprecise. Small negative λi would actually reduce the spectral radius and aid convergence; cancellation typically occurs when λi ≈ -1 (vanishing gradients) or when adding a small residual to a large signal. The logical link between the derived eigenvalue scaling, SGD step sizes, and the proposed progressive training solution needs clearer mathematical grounding.

comments.title: Misaligned Table 1 obscures independent-block ablation
comments.quote: Table 1 compares PoL’s composition of the same block versus using independent blocks with distinct weights. In spite of the much larger capacity offered by independent blocks, the noise reduction operated by Power of layers is stronger.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly claims a comparison between PoL and independent blocks, but Table 1 is misaligned in the provided text and lacks clearly delineated numerical columns for the 'independent (ind)' blocks. The header suggests POL and ind columns exist, but the data rows only clearly list values under POL or are misaligned, making it impossible to verify the central claim that PoL outperforms independent blocks despite having fewer parameters. This obscures a key ablation study that supports the weight-sharing motivation.
